elements.sent-2411-001: Calculation of the coherent nonlinear response of a system is essential to correctly interpret results from advanced techniques such as two-dimensional coherent spectroscopy (2DCS).
elements.sent-2411-002: Usually, even for the simplest systems, such calculations are either performed for low-intensity excitations where perturbative methods are valid and/or by assuming a simplified pulse envelope, such as a $\delta$-function in time.
elements.sent-2411-003: We present exact calculations using the phase-cycling method without making the aforementioned approximations.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce a generalized version of the phase-cycling method to isolate an arbitrary $N$-wave mixing signal.
elements.sent-2411-005: We then apply this method to model the saturation of the nonlinear signal from excitons in semiconductor quantum wells, which is consistent with 2DCS experiments.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also present simulation results that replicate previously-reported experiments with high-intensity excitation of semiconductor quantum dots.
elements.sent-2411-007: By accurately reproducing a variety of phenomena such as higher-order contributions, switching of coherent signal, and changes in photon-echo transients, we prove the efficacy of the phase-cycling method to calculate the coherent nonlinear signal for high-intensity excitation.
elements.sent-2411-008: This method would be particularly useful for systems with multiple, well-separated peaks and/or large inhomogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-009: Introduction Nonlinear coherent spectroscopy is a widely used technique for probing complex light-matter interactions at the quantum level \cite{Mukamel1995,Cundiff2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: A fundamental component of nonlinear spectroscopy is the wave-mixing process \cite{Shen2003,Bokor1981 , where a sequence of laser pulses interacts with the material to generate nonlinear signals based on the relevant nonlinear susceptibility of the material.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Among these interactions, four-wave mixing (FWM) is one of the most common, arising from the third-order susceptibility $\chi^{(3)}$ \cite{Vaughan2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Higher-order susceptibilities ($\chi^{(n)}$) lead to wave-mixing processes such as six-wave mixing (SWM) and beyond \cite{Zhang2013, Gibson1991, Trebino1987, Tominaga1996, Steffen1996, Lucht1992, Kang2004 , which have been used to probe the electronic \cite{Oliver2015 and vibrational states \cite{Bhattacharyya2019,Liu2019 of the system.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Building on the wave mixing principle, multidimensional coherent spectroscopy (MDCS) extends this approach by measuring the wave-mixing signal field while varying two or more time delays between interacting excitation pulses \cite{Li2023a,Oliver2018,GallagherFaeder1999,Cho2008,Brixner2017,Hamm2011,Zhang2023,Zhang2013,Zhang2012a .}
elements.sent-2411-014: By performing Fourier transforms along the axes of these time delays, MDCS disentangles the nonlinear interactions of the material across multiple frequency dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-015: This multidimensional approach offers several advantages: it measures spectrally-resolved homogeneous response, isolates individual resonances in congested spectrum, and reveals the dynamic evolution trajectories of states that would otherwise remain hidden in one-dimensional measurements.
elements.sent-2411-016: As a result, MDCS provides in-depth insights into phenomena such as energy transfer \cite{Thyrhaug2016,Bixner2012,Bressan2019 , chemical reactions \cite{Nuernberger2015 , and quantum coherence processes \cite{Butkus2017, Turner2010 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The spectra obtained from MDCS are rich in information, revealing intricate features like coherent couplings \cite{Singh2014,Li2013a,Wen2013 and many-body interactions \cite{Shacklette2003,Erementchouk2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Proper interpretation of these complex signals requires robust simulations.
elements.sent-2411-019: Conventionally, theoretical and numerical models for simulation rely on a perturbative framework, where the nonlinear response scales linearly with the electric field of each excitation pulse, for weak excitation.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this framework, Feynman diagrams (FDs) are often employed to map out specific quantum pathways \cite{Yang2007,Rose2021,Tekavec2007 , while response functions integrated with electric fields are used to compute the nonlinear response \cite{Mukamel1995,Gelin2013 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the overall response is ultimately truncated to a specific order, as it assumes the perturbative expansion remains valid \cite{Gelin2009,Gelin2003,Suess2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Consequently, MDCS experiments are generally conducted using low intensity laser pulses, ensuring that higher-order contributions remain below the noise level of measurements, keeping the analysis within the bounds of perturbation theory.
elements.sent-2411-023: On the other hand, high-intensity excitation can significantly influence coherent excitation and population transfer dynamics, as higher-order NWM processes become significant.
elements.sent-2411-024: MDCS experiments have demonstrated that intense laser field reveals various interesting phenomena across different systems, such as correlations among multiple particles \cite{Liang2021 , coherent control schemes through strong-field coupling \cite{Suzuki2016,Wen2013,Cerullo1996, Schneider2011 , coherence between highly excited states in quantum wells\cite{Turner2010 , and exciton-exciton annihilation (EEA) processes in excitonic systems \cite{Maly2018,Trinkunas2001 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, most theoretical descriptions of these phenomena rely on simplified approximations such as approximate electric-field envelopes of the excitation pulses and/or truncation of the signal up to a particular nonlinear order \cite{Anda2021,Binz2020,Bayer2009,Chen2017 , which may not be valid for all cases.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, several studies have highlighted that finite pulse durations and overlaps can affect the MDCS spectra within the perturbative regime \cite{Smallwood2017, Rose2021,Do2017,Hybl2001,Li2013,Hedse2023 , suggesting that such factors could have a significant impact in non-perturbative scenarios too.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Recently, the ability to isolate specific signals such as the third-order response even in the presence of higher-order contributions was demonstrated in transient absorption experiments \cite{Maly2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: This technique has also been extended to 2DCS experiments performed in the pump-probe geometry \cite{Luettig2023, Luettig2023a .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Collectively, these observations underscore the need for a comprehensive approach that can accurately simulate nonlinear responses in both perturbative and non-perturbative regimes without relying on simplifying assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we present a numerical model based on the phase-cycling method \cite{Tian2003,Tan2008,Zhang2012 to calculate the exact nonlinear signal without making assumptions about the pulse envelope or truncating the signal up to a specific order.}
elements.sent-2411-031: We illustrate the above capabilities through numerical solutions of the optical Bloch equations (OBEs) for a few-level quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-032: The calculations are repeated by changing the phase of the excitation pulses and summing them up with specific weights to isolate the desired nonlinear signal.
elements.sent-2411-033: We demonstrate that this approach offers exact solutions for nonlinear signals across both perturbative and nonperturbative regimes without relying on approximations of parameters such as pulse envelopes, pulse ordering, quantum pathways, and order of nonlinear interaction.
elements.sent-2411-035: We further demonstrate the usefulness of this technique by reproducing previously reported experimental findings in quantum dots through phase cycling simulations in Sec.. \ref{previous .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, we summarize our results in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-037: Nonlinear interaction of a system with a series of optical pulses results in a variety of nonlinear and linear responses.
elements.sent-2411-038: Thus, for a meaningful interpretation of the results, it is critical to isolate a particular nonlinear signal.
elements.sent-2411-039: Several techniques have been developed in order to achieve this goal both in experiments \cite{Hybl2001,Tian2003,Keusters1999,Gallagher1998 and simulations \cite{Gelin2009,GallagherFaeder1999,Mukamel2004a .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: These techniques rely on some form of phase- and/or momentum-matching condition \cite{Bristow2009,Tekavec2007 and represent a general class of wave-mixing techniques; FWM is a well-known example.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In this section, we build on the phase-cycling method of calculating FWM signals to present a generalized formalism to calculate an arbitrary NWM signal.
elements.sent-2411-042: While the discussion assumes that the light-matter interactions are perturbative in nature, in the subsequent sections we will demonstrate that the technique works even in highly nonpreturbative regime.
elements.sent-2411-043: Consider a two-level system (2LS), shown in Fig. \ref{fig:pulse (a), excited by a sequence of $M$ pulses, which are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:pulse (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The $j^{th}$ pulse arrives at time $t=t_j$ with time- and position-dependent electric field \begin{equation}\label{eq:ej} E_j(t,\vb{r}) = G_j(t-t_j)\left[ \exp{-i(\omega_j t -\vb{k}_j \cdot \vb{r} + \phi_{jM}} + c.c. \right],\end{equation} where $G_j$ is the electric field envelope, $\omega_j$ is the frequency, $\vb{k _j$ is the wavevector, $\phi_{jM}$ is the phase (defined with respect to the last pulse), and $c.c.$ denotes complex conjugate.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We consider degenerate excitation pulses and take $\omega_j = \omega_L$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The total electric field incident on the sample is \begin{equation}\label{eq:etot} E_{tot}(t, \vb{r}) = \sum_{j=1}^M E_j(t, \vb{r}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: The light-matter interaction in a 2LS can be described by the optical Bloch equations (OBEs) in the rotating-wave approximation: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}{\dot{\rho}}_{11} = -\Gamma_1 \rho_{11} - \frac{i\mu E_{tot}(t)}{\hbar} (\rho_{01}- \rho^*_{01}) {\dot{\rho}}_{01} = -(i \Delta + \gamma_{01})\rho_{01} + \frac{i\mu E_{tot}(t)}{\hbar} (\rho_{00}- \rho_{11}),\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\rho_{00}$ and $\rho_{11}$ are density matrix elements representing the population in the ground $\ket{0}$ and excited $\ket{1}$ states, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The population terms also satisfy an additional constraint for a closed system: $\rho_{00 + \rho_{11 = 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Consistent with collinear geometry, we consider all the pulses to be propagating in the same direction; thus, the $\vb{k}$, and $\vb{r}$ terms have been dropped.
elements.sent-2411-050: The solution to the OBEs gives the exact state of the 2LS after excitation with the pulse sequence.
elements.sent-2411-051: For meaningful interpretation of NWM experiment, a specific signal needs to be isolated.

The delay between $i^{th}$ and $(i+1)^{th}$ pulses is $\tau_i$; unless specified, we use this convention throughout this work.} \label{fig:pulse}
elements.sent-2411-052: A perturbative NWM signal can be seen as arising from $|\alpha_j|$-order interaction with the $j^{th}$ pulse leading to a total of $N$-order interaction with the pulse sequence.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus, \begin{equation}\label{eq:order} \sum_{j=1}^{M} |\alpha_j| = N\end{equation} and the NWM signal amplitude \begin{equation}\label{eq:amp} S_{NWM} \propto \prod_{j=1}^{M} |E_{j}|^{|\alpha_j|}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: The constants $\alpha_j$ can be either positive or negative and a collection of these define a specific $N$-wave mixing signal.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, a phase-matching condition such as \begin{equation}\label{eq:pm} \phi_{sig} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \alpha_{j}\phi_{jM}\end{equation} defines a specific NWM signal.
elements.sent-2411-056: From the constraints outlined above, it is clear that in order to efficiently set up experimental conditions or perform simulations for isolating a specific NWM signal, it is essential to determine the minimum number of pulses required.
elements.sent-2411-057: The minimum number of pulses is determined by the number of non-zero $\alpha_j$ coefficients in the phase-matching condition.
elements.sent-2411-058: For instance, in the case of a unique NWM signal where each pulse contributes to a single interaction, a minimum of $M = N$ excitation pulses are necessary to isolate the signal.
elements.sent-2411-059: In cases where pulses interact more than once, the required number of pulses M can be reduced.
elements.sent-2411-060: Energy and momentum conservation are ensured through an additional constraint on the parameters $\alpha_j$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:constraint} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \alpha_{j} = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: Consistent with the usual description of the collinear excitation scheme, the last pulse acts as a read-out pulse, which means \begin{equation}\label{eq:readout} \alpha_M = -1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: This phase matching condition is equivalent to signal emitted in the direction $\vb{k _{sig = \sum_{j=1 ^{M-1}\alpha_j \vb{k _j$ for noncollinear excitation schemes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We will next outline the phase-cycling method to calculate the NWM signal $S_{NWM}$, which is essentially equal to the population $\rho_{11}$ obtained while satisfying the phase-matching signal in Eq. \eqref{eq:pm .}
elements.sent-2411-064: The phase $\phi_{jM}$ is varied in equal steps within the range $[0, 2\pi)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: For an arbitrary NWM signal, $\alpha_j$ can take $N+1$ possible values in the range $\left[-N/2, N/2\right]$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Thus, $N+1$ phase-cycling steps for each excitation pulse is sufficient, although not necessarily optimal, to isolate any specific signal of interest.
elements.sent-2411-067: However, for a generalized description, we consider that the phase of the $j^{th}$ excitation pulse is cycled in $P_j$ steps with the total number of phase-cycling steps \begin{equation}\label{eq:pcstep} N_{PC} = \prod_{j=1}^{M-1} P_j.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-068: The phase of the read-out pulse is fixed as 0, i.e. $P_M=1$.
elements.sent-2411-069: A particular phase-cycling step $PC_r$ is defined by the collection of individual phases of the excitation pulses \begin{equation}\left( \frac{2\pi p_1}{P_1}, \frac{2\pi p_2}{P_2},\ldots ,\frac{2\pi p_{M-1}}{P_{M-1}} \right)\end{equation} where integer $p_j \in [0, P_j)$.
elements.sent-2411-070: For each phase-cycling step, we solve the OBEs in Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-071: The NWM signal is obtained through a weighted sum of the above solutions: \begin{equation}\label{eq:nwm} S_{NWM}(\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_{M-1}) = \sum_{r=1}^{N_{PC}} W_r \: \rho_{11}(\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_{M-1}, r)\end{equation} with weights \begin{equation}W_r = \prod_{j=1}^{M-1} \exp{-i\alpha_j \phi_{jM}(r)}\end{equation} and phase of the $j^{th}$ pulse in the $r^{th}$ phase-cycling step is denoted by $\phi_{jM (r)$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: In the above discussion we have assumed that the phase-cycling steps for each excitation pulse $P_j$ is known.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, the choice of the optimal phase-cycling scheme with minimum $N_{PC}$ is not obvious and needs to be determined.
elements.sent-2411-074: Such a phase-cycling scheme must ensure that aliasing of other signal does not happen \cite{Tan2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: We outline a generalized algorithm to perform the above check in Appendix \ref{app:PCM_valid .}
elements.sent-2411-076: This aliasing check can be performed as the values of $P_j$ are varied to obtain the optimal phase-cycling scheme computationally.
elements.sent-2411-077: We have made an implicit assumption in obtaining the NWM signal -- we have assumed that our 2LS is homogeneously broadened, i.e. there is a single resonance energy.
elements.sent-2411-078: However, real systems often have inhomogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-079: Let's assume that the inhomogeneous distribution is defined by function $q(\omega_m)$.
elements.sent-2411-080: The NWM signal for this inhomogeneous system can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:inhom} \begin{split} S_{NWM,in} (\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_{M-1}) = \sum_m q(\omega_m)S_{NWM}(\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_{M-1}, \omega_m), \end{split}\end{equation}where we have added explicit dependence of the homogeneous signal $S_{NWM}$ on the resonance energy $\omega_m$ \cite{Kumar2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-086: Finally, we note that the procedure to calculate the NWM signal that we have discussed in this section did not make any approximations regarding the order of interaction, quantum pathways and pulse overlap.
elements.sent-2411-087: Thus, we posit that the phase-cycling procedure can be used to calculate the coherent nonlinear response of the system even in the nonperturbative regime, as long as the equations describing the light-matter interactions are valid.
elements.sent-2411-088: In this respect, this procedure should directly mimic experimental condition, where the nonlinear signal in a particular direction or following a particular phase-matching condition can be detected even when the experiments are performed with intense excitation pulses.
elements.sent-2411-089: As a consequence we can explain results from high-intensity experiments by simply increasing the intensity of excitation pulses.

elements.section-2411-004: Phase-Cycling Simulations of Previous Experimental Works
elements.sent-2411-139: In this section, we aim to further demonstrate the effectiveness of the phase-cycling method by reproducing several results from previously reported high-intensity coherent nonlinear spectroscopy studies performed on InAs self-assembled QDs.
elements.sent-2411-140: We start each subsection with a brief description of the results that are relevant for this work.
elements.sent-2411-141: We then reproduce these results without making approximations about the pulse envelope or truncating the signal to a specific perturbation order.
elements.sent-2411-142: In addition to proving the validity of the phase-cycling method in the high-intensity regime, we also provide a pathway to correctly model the response of a system where the above approximations may not be valid.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics{Biexciton.pdf} \caption{An energy-level diagram for the exciton-biexciton diamond-type system depicting the ground state $\ket{G}$, the lowest-energy orthogonal exciton states $\ket{H}$ and $\ket{V}$, and the bound biexciton state $\ket{B}$, which is red-shifted from the unbound two-exciton state by the biexciton binding energy $\Delta_{XX}$.} \label{fig:bx}

elements.sent-2411-202: Collectively, the results presented in this section highlight the effectiveness of the phase-cycling method to calculate the exact nonlinear response even for high-intensity excitation pulses.
elements.sent-2411-203: In this work we have presented a comprehensive study on the application of the phase-cycling method to simulate coherent nonlinear signal for high-intensity excitation pulses.
elements.sent-2411-204: We started with a generalized description of the phase-cycling method that can be applied to an arbitrary NWM signal.
elements.sent-2411-205: Following this, we used the phase-cycling method to reproduce the saturation of the FWM signal as the excitation pulse intensity is increased.
elements.sent-2411-206: Finally, we reproduced three previously-reported experimental results on measuring coherent nonlinear response of QDs using high-intensity excitation pulses.
elements.sent-2411-207: This study highlights the advanced capabilities of the phase-cycling method in providing exact solutions for nonlinear signal.
elements.sent-2411-208: Unlike conventional approaches that often rely on simplifying assumptions, such as the use of Feynman diagrams to consider relevant quantum pathways, or assumptions about pulse shapes, the phase-cycling method handles all these complexities inherently.
elements.sent-2411-209: Prior studies have have attempted to address limitations of the conventional perturbative framework by introducing finite pulse solutions to examine effects such as narrow spectral bandwidths \cite{Abramavicius2010,Leng2017,Schweigert2008 , chirp \cite{Smallwood2017,Binz2020 , and pulse overlap \cite{Hybl2001,Li2013,Hedse2023 on the total response.}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: However, these methods typically address a single factor at a time and still rely on truncating the response at a specific order.
elements.sent-2411-211: In contrast, the phase-cycling approach is effective across both perturbative and non-perturbative regimes, allowing us to simulate these pulse effects accurately without the need for such approximations.. .
elements.sent-2411-212: The phase-cycling method involves repeated measurements/calculations of the nonlinear signal for varying phase of the excitation pulses.
elements.sent-2411-213: Consequently, an increase in the number of phase-cycling steps results in an increase in the total time for the simulation/experiment.
elements.sent-2411-214: Thus, it is imperative to find the optimal phase-cycling scheme that uses minimum number of phase-cycling steps while successfully isolating the desired signal.
elements.sent-2411-215: An essential step is to ensure that the chosen phase-cycling scheme works for the desired signal without aliasing \cite{Tan2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: We present an algorithm to validate the phase-cycling scheme defined by Eq. \eqref{eq:pcstep for isolating the NWM signal under the phase matching condition defined by Eq. \eqref{eq:pm .}}
elements.sent-2411-217: We list all possible nonlinear signals up to $N^{th}$ order light-matter interaction.
elements.sent-2411-218: We do this by finding all possible combinations of $\alpha'_j$ that satisfy the constraints defined by Eq. \eqref{eq:constraint and \begin{equation}\sum_{j=1}^{M-1} \abs{\alpha'_{j}} + 1 \leq N. \label{eq:cond2}\end{equation} The first condition ensures that the system necessarily ends in a population state after the last excitation pulse.}
elements.sent-2411-219: The second constraint ensures that we include only the contributions up to the $N^{th}$ order, including lower-order terms.
elements.sent-2411-220: We list signals originating due to aliasing of the required phase-matched signal, as defined by Eq. \eqref{eq:pm .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Specifically, we find all combinations of $\alpha_j + I P_j$, with integer $I \in [0,N)$.
elements.sent-2411-222: We have chosen the range of $I$ to include all relevant aliased signals.
elements.sent-2411-223: Finally, we check for validity of phase-cycling scheme by comparing the two lists.. \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-224: If only one element is common between the two lists (corresponding to the required signal), the phase-cycling scheme is valid and successfully isolates the NWM signal.
elements.sent-2411-225: If more than one element is common, it means that some aliased signals will mix with the desired signal, indicating that the phase-cycling scheme does not uniquely isolate the required signal.
elements.sent-2411-226: Thus, the phase cycling scheme is invalid.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-227: We can iteratively increase the number of phase-cycling steps for each pulse $P_j \in [2,N+1]$ while using the above the algorithm to check whether it will work for the required signal.
elements.sent-2411-228: The scheme with the minimum value of $N_{PC}$ that satisfies the above algorithm can be chosen as the optimum phase-cycling scheme.
elements.sent-2411-229: This optimization process can be automated using a computer program to find the optimized phase-cycling scheme for any arbitrary NWM signal.
